name: RABBIT-CAGE-BUG-10
title: settings.json on scope-guard basename allowlist lets agents bypass scope protection for repo config
description:
scope-guard.py line 96 includes 'settings.json' in its blanket filename allowlist:

    if base in ("settings.json", "settings.local.json", ".gitignore", ".rabbit-scope-override"):
        return True, "ALLOW (allowlisted filename)"

This means any file named settings.json anywhere in the repo — including .claude/settings.json — bypasses scope checking entirely, regardless of whether any scope marker is active.

Observed failure: in a session without any active scope marker, an agent edited .claude/settings.json (adding Write(*) and Edit(*) to permissions) without triggering a scope-guard denial. The edit went through because the basename 'settings.json' matched the allowlist.

Root cause: settings.json was added to the allowlist to allow frictionless permission edits, but this exempts the repo's canonical Claude Code config from the same discipline applied to all other feature files. .claude/settings.json is owned by rabbit-cage and changes to it should require an active rabbit-cage scope marker.

Secondary behavioral failure: the agent did not recognize that editing .claude/settings.json (Claude Code permissions/hooks surface) requires rabbit-feature-touch targeting rabbit-cage. It treated it as a trivial config tweak.

Fix: remove 'settings.json' from the basename allowlist in scope-guard.py. settings.local.json may remain (it is user-local and not checked in). Writes to .claude/settings.json should require an active rabbit-cage scope marker like any other rabbit-cage file.

history.note: TDD cycle complete. Removed settings.json from scope-guard basename allowlist; writes to .claude/settings.json now require an active rabbit-cage scope marker. 6 new e2e tests added (BUG-10 DENY, scope-marker ALLOW, regression for remaining allowlist entries, source-level assertion).
